California
Fair Political
Practices Cornmission

Marguerite P. Battersby
City Attorney, Yucaipa
Brunick, Alvarez & Battersby
1839 Connercenter West
P. O. Box 6425
San Bernardino, cA 92412
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-91-3 2 0
Dear Ms. Battersby:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of Yucaipa City councilmember Gary Pitts regarding her
responsibilities pursuant to the conf I ict-of- interest provisions
or tne Poriticar ileforrn act.1
OUESTION
May councilmetnber Pitts participate in Yucaipa city council
decisions concerning a developer who has applied for aPprovaL for
a 370-unit developnent project where the developer is a source of
income to her feed store in excess of 5250 in the past 12 rnonths?
CONCLUS ION
Councilnenber Pitts may not participate in the Yucaipa City
Council's consideration of a proposed 37O-unj't development project
if the applicant has been a source of income to the
counc i l:nember' s feed store in excess of S250 in the past 12
nonths.
FACTS
According to your facts, Councilrnenber Gary Pitts is
currently a Yucaipa city councilmernber and jointly operates, with
her spouse, a feed store in the jurisdiction. 
The population of
1 Gor".rr."nt code Sections 81ooo-91o15. All statutory references
are to the Governnent code unless otherr,rise indicated.

regulations appear at 2 california Code of Regulations Section

2
Division 5 of the California Code of Regulati.ons.

-320

the city is approxirnately 32,oo0. There are three other feed
stores in Vucaipa. You stated that approxinately 10 percent of
the residents of the city may frequent the four feed stores.
councilnember Pitts estiruates that she has 300 to 350 regular
customers but cannot accurately estimate the total number of
custoners who may sporadicatly visit the store. Total annual
sales for her store average S35o,0oo to S4oo,o00.
The city council is considering a 37o-unit project proposed
for 670 acres of land in and near the city Linits' 
Necessary
approvals include a pre-annexation agreenent, annexation,
divelopment agreernent, and other land use pernits required-by city
ordina-nce. iou anticipate that the first application will be
heard by the citY at the end of JUIY.
Over the past month, the applicant for the development made
two large purcilases fron the councilmember's store, aqgregating
S1,1OO.- Tire developer also asked the councilnember to oPen an
account with the counc i Lrnenber, s store, which the councilmember
declined. The councilmember's store does not naintain accounts
for any of her custoners. You also stated that the councilnember
does tire daily sales and bookkeeping of the business but has no
nethod for tricking custoners, other than by their unpaid bilIs.
ANALYSI S
SectionsTloooftheActprohibitsanypublicofficialfrom
rnaking, participating in making, or othervise using.his.or her
"iii.iif'posititn 
to influence a governnental decision in which
the official 
has a financial inteiest' 
A "public official" 
is
aefinea in Section 82048 and Regulation 18700 as every natural
l"ii"" 
who is a mernber, officer, enployee, or consultant of a
'=tut" oI. local government agency and would include Yucaipa city
councilmembers.
Econonic Interests
Section 87103 specifies that a pubJ'ic official 
has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision wiII have a material financial effect,
distinguishable fron the effect on the public generally, on the
officiit 
or a nenber of his or her imnediate family or on:
(c) Any source of income, other than gifts and
other'than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official 
status, aggregating tlro hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or nore in value provided to,
received by or pronised to the public official'
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

generally if
consequently′  any person or buSiness that has made a payment
to the councilmember is a source of income to the councilmember.

store in the jurisdiction.  SeCtiOn 82030 provides that the income

interest purposes of the Act.  Th
is that if an individual customer iS nOt distinguishable from
every other customer′
 the like■ihOod Of faVOritism is reduced.
Regulation 18703.5 whiCh interprets Section 87103.5 provides
in a pertinent part:
DurDoses of Government code section
r;taiI 
custoners of a business entity
signif j.cant seginent of the publ'ic
eiaher of the following is true:
(1) The retail customers of the business
entity during the preceding 12 months are
suffiiient 
in nunber to equal 10 percent or
more of the population or households of the
jurisdiction; 
or
(2) The retail customers of the business
entity during the preceding 12 nonths number
at least ten thousand.
For purposes of this subdivision, a customer
ot a ieiuir tusiness entity is each separate and


Our File No. A-9■
-320
Page 4
distinct purchaser of goods or services, whether an
individual, household, business or other entity.
rf records are not naintained by custoner name, a
good faith estirnate shatl be nade to determine what
percentage of sales transactions represent nultiple
transactions by rePeat customers- The total number
of sales transactions shall then be reduced by the
estimated percentage of repeat custoners to yield
the nunber of customers for purposes of applying
this subdivision.
The threshold requirement of the exception is that the retail
custotners of the official,s 
business over the preceding 12 loonths
constitute 10 percent or more of the population or households in
the jurisdiction or number at teast ten thousand. You stated that
10 p6rcent of the poPulation of Yucaipa frequent the four feed
stores in the juriidiction. 
consequently, in any case, it- would
be unlikely th;t ro percent or more of the population of the
jurisdiction would fiequent the councilnember's store over the
6o,rrse of the last 12 rnonths. l,toreover. since only approximately
3,200 persons would frequent aLl. the feed stores, it is irnpossible
for 10,ooo persons to shop at the councilnenber's store' 
Since
the customeis of Councilmlmber Pitts, store are not a significant
segment of the jurisdiction, 
the exception in 87103'5 does not
apP Iy .
Foreseeable Material Financial Effects on Economic Interests
whether the financial consequences of a decision are
reasonably foreseeable at the time a governnental decision is rnade
depends on th" facts of each particular case. An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it wiII occur. certainty is not required'
However, if an effect is only a nere possibility, 
it is not
r"a=onaily foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC ops' 198')
In addition, the effect on the councilnenber's econohic
interest rnust be naterial in order to require disqual if icat ion '
The Comnission has adopted differing guidelines to determine
whether an effect is rnaterial, depending on the specific
circunstances of each decision. For example, where a source of
income is directly before .the city council as an applicant or the
subject of the decision, fiegulation la702- 1(a) provides that the
eff6ct of the decision on the source of income is deerned material
and disqual if ication is required. (Regulation 18702.1(b); conbs
Advice Letter, No. A-89-177. )
Thus, since the developer is a source of incone and the
applicant with respect to the decisions in question, the decision
will have a naterial financial effect on the councilnenber's
econonic interest.


PubIic cenerallv Exception
Finally, even if the councilnenber has an economic interest
that will be naterially affected by the decision, she may still
participate in the decision if the effect on the counci lrnenber's
interest is not distinguishabte from the effect on the public
general,Iy. For the rrpublic generallyrr exception to app1y, the
decision must affect the source of incorne in substantially the
same nanner as it wilI affect a significant segTment of Yucaipa.
(Regulation 18703.) However, it appears unlikely that a decision
con-erning the developer's project wilL affect the a significant
segment oi the population of Yucaipa in the same manner as the
decision wiII affect the developer.
Therefore, we conclude that the councilnember is disqualified
from participation in decisions relating to the developer's
applilation until rZ nonths have elapsed following the payment of
income of S25o or more to the counci lmernber' s business.
Participation
Please note that participation in governmental decisions has
been interpreted broadly in furtherance of the goal's of the Act.
Thus, wheri the councilmember is disqualified with respect to
planning comnission decisions, she is also prohibited from naking
tontacti with, appearing before, or otherwise attempting to
j.nf luence. any menber, officer, employee or consultant of your
agency concerning the governnental decision. (Regulations 18700
ana lazoo.f.) 
nowever, Councilrnember Pitts nay connunicate with
the general public or the press. (Regulation 18700.1(b)(5); see
also, Holrnes Advice Letter, No. A-88-471; Hinel'ine Advice Letter,
No. A-88-149, copies enclosed. )
